name: Ship of Theseus
blurb: Swap every plank of a ship over the years and it sails on, same ship, or a new one wearing its old name?

name: Hilbert's Hotel
blurb: A hotel with infinitely many full rooms can still fit one more guest: just ask everyone to shuffle down a door.

name: Gabriel's Horn
blurb: An endless trumpet shape holds only a cupful of volume, yet its surface stretches on without any limit.

name: The Painter's Paradox
blurb: You could fill that same horn with a finite splash of paint, yet no amount of paint could ever coat its inside.

name: Gabriel's Wedding Cake
blurb: Stack forever-shrinking layers into a cake of finite volume but infinite frosting to spread.

name: The Coastline
blurb: Measure a coast with a shorter ruler and it grows longer; keep zooming in and its length has no end.

name: Banach-Tarski
blurb: Cut a solid ball into a few strange pieces, rotate them, and rebuild two balls each the size of the first.

name: Cantor's Infinities
blurb: Some infinities beat others: there are more decimals between 0 and 1 than there are counting numbers.

name: The Potato Paradox
blurb: A 100-pound sack of potatoes is 99% water; let it dry to 98% water and it now weighs just 50 pounds.

name: Coupon Collector
blurb: To collect all ten cereal-box prizes you'll buy about thirty boxes; the last few hide the longest.

name: Birthday Paradox
blurb: Gather just 23 people and it's already more likely than not that two of them share a birthday.

name: Interesting Numbers
blurb: Every number is interesting, because the very first boring one would be fascinating for exactly that reason.

name: The Staircase
blurb: Zigzag steps shrink toward a straight diagonal, yet their total length stays stubbornly longer than the line.

name: Thomson's Lamp
blurb: Flip a lamp on, off, on, twice as fast each time; after one minute, is it glowing or dark?

name: The Balls of Littlewood
blurb: Add ten balls, remove one, faster and faster forever, and whether any remain depends on which you remove.

name: Achilles and the Tortoise
blurb: Give a tortoise a head start and quick Achilles must first reach where it was, so how does he ever pass?

name: Zeno's Arrow
blurb: At every frozen instant a flying arrow sits perfectly still, so where does its motion actually hide?

name: The Dichotomy
blurb: To cross a room you first cross half, then half of that, an endless list of steps before you even begin.

name: The Twin Paradox
blurb: Rocket to the stars and back and you return younger than the twin who stayed home waiting for you.

name: Olbers' Paradox
blurb: If stars fill an endless universe, every line of sight should hit one, so why isn't the whole night ablaze?

name: The Fermi Paradox
blurb: Billions of stars, billions of years, endless chances for company, so where in the cosmos is everybody?

name: The Ladder Paradox
blurb: A ladder too long for a barn fits inside once it races fast enough, as motion squeezes its very length.

name: Maxwell's Demon
blurb: A tiny gatekeeper sorting fast and slow molecules seems to make heat flow the wrong way, for free.

name: The Tea-Leaf Paradox
blurb: Stir your tea and the leaves gather in the middle, drifting against the swirl instead of flinging outward.

name: The Mpemba Effect
blurb: Under the right conditions, hot water can freeze into solid ice faster than cold water does.

name: Feather and Hammer
blurb: Drop a feather and a hammer where there's no air and they strike the ground at the very same instant.

name: The Ehrenfest Disk
blurb: Spin a disk fast enough and its rim shrinks while its width stays put, bending the circle's own geometry.

name: Bell's Spaceships
blurb: Two ships accelerate side by side, yet the delicate string between them snaps, stretched by motion itself.

name: The Liar
blurb: 'This sentence is false.' If it's true then it's false, and if it's false then it's true, round and round.

name: The Card Paradox
blurb: One card says the back is true; the back says the front is false, each side quietly undoing the other.

name: Curry's Paradox
blurb: A single self-referring sentence can seem to prove any wild claim you like, just by talking about itself.

name: Berry's Paradox
blurb: 'The smallest number not nameable in under twelve words', which you just named using only eleven.

name: The Heterological
blurb: Does the word 'long' describe itself? Now ask that of 'not-self-describing' and watch the answer break.

name: Quine's Paradox
blurb: 'Yields falsehood when preceded by its quotation' yields falsehood when preceded by its quotation.

name: The Barber
blurb: A barber shaves everyone who doesn't shave himself, so who on earth gets to shave the barber?

name: Russell's Set
blurb: Picture the set of all sets that don't contain themselves; does it contain itself? Either answer breaks.

name: The Crocodile
blurb: A crocodile promises to return a child if you guess its plan, so guess it won't, and watch it freeze up.

name: Protagoras' Court
blurb: A teacher's fee is due once the student wins a case, so their very first case is over that fee, and loops.

name: Yablo's Paradox
blurb: An endless list of lines, each saying 'every line below is false', yet not one can be true or false.

name: The Preface
blurb: An author believes every sentence in the book, yet admits somewhere a mistake surely hides, both reasonable.

name: Moore's Paradox
blurb: 'It's raining, but I don't believe it is': each half can be true, yet no one can sincerely say the whole.

name: Monty Hall
blurb: Pick one of three doors, then switch after a losing one opens, and your odds of winning quietly double.

name: Simpson's Paradox
blurb: A trend can hold in every single group, yet flip completely the moment you pool those groups together.

name: The Two Envelopes
blurb: One envelope holds twice the other; whichever you pick, switching seems to promise more, and never stops.

name: Bertrand's Box
blurb: Draw a gold coin from a random box, and the chance the other coin is gold isn't half but two in three.

name: St. Petersburg
blurb: A coin game with an infinite average payout, so why would almost no one pay much to sit down and play?

name: Newcomb's Paradox
blurb: A predictor who's never wrong sets the boxes; take one or both, and two flawless logics disagree forever.

name: Braess's Paradox
blurb: Add a shiny new road to a busy city and, astonishingly, everyone's drive home can get slower.

name: The Ellsberg Paradox
blurb: People happily bet on known odds over unknown ones, even when the mystery jar might pay far better.

name: Parrondo's Paradox
blurb: Alternate between two games you're each guaranteed to lose, and you can somehow come out ahead.

name: The Friendship Paradox
blurb: On average, your friends have more friends than you do, and it's true for almost everyone at once.

name: Jevons Paradox
blurb: Make engines more efficient and we don't burn less fuel; we use so much more that total use climbs.

name: Diamond and Water
blurb: Water keeps us alive and diamonds do almost nothing, yet the useless gem costs a small fortune, why?

name: The Abilene Paradox
blurb: A whole group takes a trip nobody wanted, each going along to please the others, who felt the exact same.

name: The Peter Principle
blurb: People keep getting promoted until they land in a job they're finally bad at, and there they stay.

name: The Streisand Effect
blurb: Try hard to hide something and you summon a crowd; the hushing is exactly what makes everyone look.

name: The Observer's Paradox
blurb: To study how people truly talk you must watch them, but being watched changes how they talk.

name: The Heap
blurb: Take one grain from a heap of sand and it's still a heap, so when exactly does it stop being one?

name: The Bald Man
blurb: Pluck one hair and no one turns bald; keep going and a full head becomes bare, but at which single hair?

name: Map and Territory
blurb: The most perfect map of a land would have to be the land itself, so no map can ever be truly complete.

name: Heraclitus' River
blurb: Step into a river twice and it's fresh water each time, so can you ever enter the same river again?

name: The Dream Argument
blurb: Everything feels solid and real right now, but so does a dream, right up until the moment you wake.

name: The Stone
blurb: Could a power great enough to lift anything at all create a stone that even it could not lift?

name: Meno's Paradox
blurb: How do you search for something new, when you wouldn't recognize the answer until you'd already found it?

name: The Learner's Paradox
blurb: You can't learn what you already know, nor what you don't, so how does anyone learn anything at all?

name: The Experience Machine
blurb: A machine could feed you a lifetime of perfect joy, so why does something in us still want the real thing?

name: Sleeping Beauty
blurb: Woken once or twice by a coin you never see, how sure should you be it landed heads: one-half or one-third?

name: The Copernican Principle
blurb: You're probably nowhere special in time or space, a humbling clue to how vast the cosmos runs beyond you.

name: The Boltzmann Brain
blurb: In an endless cosmos, stray atoms might now and then assemble a full mind, dreaming up an entire world.

name: Chronology Protection
blurb: The universe may quietly forbid every time-loop, as if nature itself stands guard over the past.

name: Aristotle's Wheel
blurb: A big wheel and a small one fixed together roll the same distance, so do their two circles somehow match?

name: Galileo's Paradox
blurb: There are exactly as many perfect squares as whole numbers, even though most numbers aren't squares.

name: The Dartboard
blurb: A thrown dart is sure to land on the board, yet the chance of hitting any one exact point is precisely zero.

name: Skolem's Paradox
blurb: Math proves some infinities are uncountably vast, yet its own rules fit inside a tiny countable world.

name: Berkeley's Ghosts
blurb: Early calculus treated tiny amounts as both something and nothing, 'ghosts of departed quantities' that worked.

name: Grandi's Series
blurb: Add 1 minus 1 plus 1 minus 1 forever: group it one way you get zero, another way you get one.

name: The Koch Snowflake
blurb: A snowflake curve you could draw inside a coin, yet whose crinkled edge is infinitely long.

name: The Menger Sponge
blurb: Keep drilling holes in a cube forever and you're left with infinite surface but no volume at all.

name: The Cantor Dust
blurb: Erase the middle third again and again, and infinitely many points survive while taking up no length.

name: The Devil's Staircase
blurb: A staircase that climbs steadily from bottom to top while staying flat almost everywhere it goes.

name: The Weierstrass Curve
blurb: A line so jagged it's unbroken everywhere yet has a sharp corner at every single point along it.

name: The Space-Filling Curve
blurb: One bent, one-dimensional thread can be made to pass through every point of a solid square.

name: Godel's Incompleteness
blurb: In any rich system of math there are true statements it can never prove using its own rules.

name: The Halting Problem
blurb: No program can ever exist that reliably tells whether every other program will finish or loop forever.

name: The Endless Nines
blurb: The number 0.999... with nines running on forever isn't merely close to 1, it is exactly and provably 1.

name: The Impossible Sum
blurb: Add 1 plus 2 plus 3 forever and a strange, deep kind of math hands you back negative one-twelfth.

name: The Missing Square
blurb: Rearrange the same four puzzle pieces and an empty hole appears from nowhere, so where did the area go?

name: The Pigeonhole
blurb: Somewhere in a big city two strangers have the exact same number of hairs on their heads, guaranteed.

name: The Mobius Strip
blurb: Give a paper loop a single half-twist and it becomes a surface with only one side and one edge.

name: The Klein Bottle
blurb: A bottle whose inside is its outside, a vessel that holds nothing because it has no real insides.

name: The Hairy Ball
blurb: You can't comb a hairy ball perfectly flat; there's always a cowlick, and on Earth, a calm spot of wind.

name: Brouwer's Fixed Point
blurb: Stir your coffee all you like and, once it settles, at least one drop ends up right where it started.

name: Borsuk-Ulam
blurb: At every moment two spots on exact opposite sides of Earth share the same temperature and air pressure.

name: The Ham Sandwich
blurb: One straight cut can perfectly halve the bread, the cheese, and the filling all at once, however they lie.

name: The Pizza Theorem
blurb: Cut a pizza in eight through any off-center point, and two friends taking alternate slices get equal shares.

name: The Napkin Ring
blurb: Bore a hole of equal height through a marble or a planet and the leftover rings share the same volume.

name: Borromean Rings
blurb: Three rings can lock together so cleverly that removing any one frees the other two completely.

name: Sphere Eversion
blurb: A hollow sphere can be turned inside out through itself, smoothly, without a single crease or tear.

name: The Raven Paradox
blurb: 'All ravens are black' is quietly supported by every green apple, since each is a non-black non-raven.

name: The Grue Riddle
blurb: What if gems are 'grue', green until New Year then blue? Nothing you've seen so far could rule it out.

name: The Lottery Paradox
blurb: It's reasonable to believe any single ticket will lose, yet unreasonable to believe every ticket will.

name: The Surprise Quiz
blurb: A teacher promises a surprise quiz next week; clever students prove it's impossible, then it surprises them.

name: The Two Generals
blurb: Two allies must attack together, but no messenger can ever confirm that the last message got through.

name: Buridan's Donkey
blurb: Place a donkey exactly between two identical meals, equally tempting, and pure logic gives it no way to choose.

name: The Toxin Puzzle
blurb: You're paid a fortune just to intend to sip a harmless toxin tomorrow, but can you intend what you'll never do?

name: The Chicken and Egg
blurb: The first chicken hatched from an egg, but that egg was laid by a chicken, so which could possibly come first?

name: Pinocchio's Nose
blurb: If Pinocchio says 'my nose grows now', does it? If it grows he told the truth; if not, he lied and it should.

name: Socratic Ignorance
blurb: The wisest person may simply be the one who best knows just how much they still don't know.

name: The Paradox of Analysis
blurb: A perfect definition tells you precisely what a word means, yet if it's exactly right it teaches you nothing new.

name: The Paradox of Fiction
blurb: We weep for characters we know were never real, so how can wholly made-up people move us so genuinely?

name: The Paradox of Suspense
blurb: A film can grip you with suspense on the tenth rewatch, even though you already know how it all ends.

name: Fitch's Paradox
blurb: If every truth could in principle be known, then strangely it follows that every truth is already known.

name: The Placebo Effect
blurb: A plain sugar pill with no medicine can genuinely ease real pain, simply because you believe that it will.

name: The Pygmalion Effect
blurb: Expect more from someone and they often rise to meet it, belief quietly reshaping what they can actually do.

name: The Hawthorne Effect
blurb: People work differently the very moment they know they're being watched, so watching changes the result.

name: The Frequency Illusion
blurb: Learn a new word today and suddenly it's everywhere; the world didn't change, only your attention did.

name: Dunning and Kruger
blurb: The less someone knows about a skill, the more likely they are to wildly overrate how good they are at it.

name: The Red Queen
blurb: In nature you must keep evolving as fast as you can just to hold your ground against everything else evolving.

name: The Cobra Effect
blurb: Pay people to catch cobras and they'll breed cobras for the reward, an incentive that creates what it fights.

name: The Icarus Paradox
blurb: The very strengths that lift a company to the top are often what carry it too far and bring it back down.

name: The Paradox of Choice
blurb: Offer someone twenty jams instead of three and they're less likely to buy, and less happy if they do.

name: The Paradox of Thrift
blurb: When everyone saves at once, spending dries up, and the whole economy can end up poorer for it.

name: Giffen Goods
blurb: For a few staple foods, raising the price can make people buy even more of them rather than less.

name: The Easterlin Paradox
blurb: Once basic needs are met, a richer country isn't reliably a happier one over the long run.

name: The Productivity Puzzle
blurb: Computers landed on every desk, yet for years the promised boom barely showed up in the numbers.

name: Tragedy of the Commons
blurb: Share a pasture and each herder gains by adding one more animal, until the grass everyone needs is gone.

name: The Prisoner's Dilemma
blurb: Two partners each do better by turning on the other, yet if both stay loyal, both come out further ahead.

name: Condorcet's Paradox
blurb: A group can prefer A to B, B to C, and C to A, so the majority's will loops with no clear winner.

name: Arrow's Theorem
blurb: No voting system can turn everyone's honest rankings into a group choice that obeys every fair rule at once.

name: Nontransitive Dice
blurb: Three dice can be built so the first usually beats the second, the second the third, and the third the first.

name: Penney's Game
blurb: In a coin-flip race between two three-flip patterns, whoever picks second can always tilt the odds their way.

name: The Two Children
blurb: 'One of my two kids is a boy': the chance the other is a girl isn't half but a surprising two in three.

name: The False Positive
blurb: Test positive for a rare disease with a great test, and you're still probably fine; the rarity outweighs it.

name: Berkson's Paradox
blurb: Among the people you meet, two unrelated traits can look linked purely because of who ended up in the room.

name: The Inspection Paradox
blurb: Your wait for the bus feels long because you're more likely to arrive during a longer-than-average gap.

name: Will Rogers Effect
blurb: Move the sickest patient from one ward to another and the average health of both wards can rise at once.

name: Benford's Law
blurb: In heaps of real-world numbers, far more begin with a 1 than a 9, a hidden bias baked into the digits.

name: The Lindy Effect
blurb: The longer a book, idea, or tool has already survived, the longer you can expect it to keep going.

name: Allais Paradox
blurb: People flip their choice between the same two gambles based only on how the odds happen to be dressed up.

name: Bertrand's Chord
blurb: Ask for a 'random' chord of a circle and the odds it's long depend entirely on what 'random' even means.

name: Schrodinger's Cat
blurb: Sealed in a box with a hidden switch, a cat is somehow both asleep and awake, until the instant you peek.

name: Quantum Entanglement
blurb: Measure one of two linked particles and its partner answers instantly, even a galaxy away, spookily so.

name: The Quantum Zeno
blurb: Watch a quantum system closely enough and your constant looking can freeze it; the watched pot never boils.

name: Wheeler's Delayed Choice
blurb: How you choose to measure light now seems to decide whether it acted as wave or particle moments ago.

name: The Uncertainty Principle
blurb: The sharper you pin down where a particle is, the blurrier how fast it moves becomes, by nature's design.

name: The Arrow of Time
blurb: Physics runs the same forwards or backwards, yet cups shatter and never unshatter, so where is 'forward'?

name: The Black Hole Puzzle
blurb: Toss a book into a black hole and its information seems erased, yet deep law says nothing is ever truly lost.

name: The Stretching Band
blurb: A tiny ant crawls along a rubber band that stretches longer every second, yet somehow still reaches the end.

name: The Brazil Nut Effect
blurb: Shake a can of mixed nuts and the biggest ones climb to the top, defying the downward pull of gravity.

name: Feynman's Sprinkler
blurb: A sprinkler spins one way pushing water out, but if it instead sucks water in, which way does it turn?

name: The Archer's Paradox
blurb: An arrow must bend around the very bow it's shot from, wobbling in flight yet flying straight to the mark.

name: D'Alembert's Paradox
blurb: In a perfect, frictionless fluid a moving object would feel no drag at all, so how does anything slow down?

name: The Faint Young Sun
blurb: Billions of years ago the sun shone far dimmer, yet early Earth stayed warm enough for liquid oceans, why?

name: The Goldilocks Universe
blurb: Nudge a few cosmic constants a hair and stars, planets, and life vanish; ours sits improbably just right.

name: The Horizon Problem
blurb: Opposite edges of the sky share the same temperature, though they seem too far apart to have ever met.

name: The Simulation Argument
blurb: If a future civilization could run vast, perfect world-simulations, how sure are you that you aren't in one?

name: Gibbs' Paradox
blurb: Mix two jars of the same gas and nothing really changes, but mix two different gases and disorder jumps, why?

name: The Bootstrap Paradox
blurb: A traveler carries a song to the past, where it's copied and handed forward to be carried back; who wrote it?

name: The Unstoppable Force
blurb: What happens when an unstoppable force meets an immovable object? If both truly exist, the words undo themselves.

name: The Paradox of Voting
blurb: One vote almost never decides an election, so by cold logic why bother? And yet millions happily do.

name: The Paradox of Hedonism
blurb: Chase happiness head-on and it slips away; it tends to arrive as a side effect of chasing something else.

name: The Paradox of Automation
blurb: The better our machines run themselves, the rustier our own skills grow for the rare moment they fail.

name: Moravec's Paradox
blurb: For computers, hard chess is easy while a toddler's easy tasks, seeing and walking, are staggeringly hard.

name: Polanyi's Paradox
blurb: We know how to ride a bike or recognize a face, yet can't fully spell out the rules we're actually following.

name: The Uncanny Valley
blurb: A robot face grows more charming as it nears human, then, just short of real, it turns oddly unsettling.

name: Zeno's Millet
blurb: If one falling seed makes no sound, how does a whole bushel poured out make a noise? Yet it plainly does.

name: The Sailing Paradox
blurb: A sailboat can slice along faster than the very wind that's pushing it, and even sail against that wind.

name: The Tippe Top
blurb: Spin a rounded little top and it flips itself upside down, rising to balance improbably on its own stem.

name: The Rattleback
blurb: Spin this small stone one way and it spins on happily; spin it the other and it stops and reverses itself.

name: The Chain Fountain
blurb: Tip a chain out of a jar and it leaps up into an arch above the rim before falling, pouring upward first.

name: The Leidenfrost Drop
blurb: A water drop on a scorching pan skates around for ages, gliding on a thin cushion of its own steam.

name: Supercooling
blurb: Chill pure water below freezing and it stays perfectly liquid, until one tap turns it all to ice in a blink.

name: The Chinese Room
blurb: A person who knows no Chinese follows a rulebook to answer notes perfectly; do they understand, or just seem to?

name: Mary's Room
blurb: A scientist who knows every fact about color but has only seen gray learns something new the day she sees red.

name: The Beetle Box
blurb: If each of us had a private box we call 'beetle', the word could work fine even if no two boxes matched.

name: Swampman
blurb: If lightning made a perfect copy of you, atom for atom, would it be you, or a brand-new stranger with your past?

name: The Teletransporter
blurb: A machine scans you here and builds a perfect copy there, so does you step out on the far side, or a twin?

name: Gambler's Ruin
blurb: Bet again and again against a far richer player on a fair coin, and given time you'll almost surely run out.

name: The Secretary Problem
blurb: Interview hires one by one with no going back; view the first 37%, then grab the next best, to win most often.

name: The Locker Puzzle
blurb: A hundred players each open half the lockers to find their name, yet one looping trick lets nearly all win.

name: The Hat Puzzle
blurb: Wearing hats they can't see, a line of thinkers can guess their own colors almost perfectly using only parity.

name: The Elevator Paradox
blurb: Waiting near the top of a tower, the first elevator to arrive is almost always heading down, and vice versa.

name: Carroll's Tortoise
blurb: The tortoise makes Achilles justify each logic step, then justify that step, so can reasoning ever get moving?

name: Veblen Goods
blurb: Some luxuries actually sell better the pricier they get; raise the tag and, oddly, more people want to own it.

name: The Resource Curse
blurb: Nations sitting on oil or gold often grow poorer and less stable than neighbors with far less to dig up.

name: The Backfire Effect
blurb: Show people solid proof they're wrong and, sometimes, they end up clinging to the mistaken belief even harder.

name: Tocqueville's Paradox
blurb: The more conditions improve, the more the injustices that remain start to feel unbearable rather than smaller.

name: Parkinson's Law
blurb: Work quietly swells to fill exactly as much time as you set aside for it, however little was really needed.

name: Schelling's Tipping
blurb: Tiny, mild preferences in each person can snowball a whole crowd into sharp patterns nobody actually wanted.

name: The False Proof
blurb: Sneak in a division by a cleverly hidden zero and you can 'prove' that 1 equals 2, math's favorite magic trick.

name: The Coin Rotation
blurb: Roll one coin once around another of the same size and it turns twice, not once, before it's home again.

name: The Quantum Cheshire Cat
blurb: In a clever setup a particle and its own spin can travel apart, like a grin drifting off without the cat.

name: The Double Slit
blurb: Send particles one at a time through two slits and they slowly paint a wave, as if each went through both.

name: The Quantum Eraser
blurb: Wipe out the record of which path a particle took, even afterward, and its wave pattern quietly returns.

name: Bell's Theorem
blurb: No secret pre-written script can explain entangled particles; reality is stranger than any hidden plan.

name: The Ultraviolet Catastrophe
blurb: Old physics said a warm object should pour out infinite blue light; fixing that riddle gave birth to quantum theory.

name: The Vacuum Catastrophe
blurb: Theory overshoots the energy of empty space by a 1 with 120 zeros, the worst prediction ever, still unexplained.

name: The Cosmic Coincidence
blurb: Of all the ages of the cosmos, ours is the rare one where matter and dark energy nearly balance, so why now?

name: The Onion Genome
blurb: A humble onion carries about five times more DNA than you do, so genome size tells you little about a creature.

name: The Plankton Paradox
blurb: Countless plankton species crowd the same water on the same few nutrients, when theory says only a handful should.

name: The Spotlight Effect
blurb: You feel everyone clocked your tiny stumble, but they barely did; each of them stars in their own spotlight.

name: The IKEA Effect
blurb: We treasure the wobbly shelf we assembled ourselves far more than a sturdier one we could have simply bought.

name: The Endowment Effect
blurb: The instant something becomes yours it feels worth more; now you won't sell it for what you'd have paid to buy it.

name: The Decoy Effect
blurb: Slip in a third, clearly worse option and watch people drift toward the choice you quietly wanted all along.

name: The Hedonic Treadmill
blurb: Reach a bigger joy and you soon adjust to it, sliding back toward the same everyday level of happiness as before.

name: Twin Earth
blurb: On a planet where 'water' is a look-alike chemical, does the word mean the same thing, or is meaning outside us?

name: The Nation of Minds
blurb: If a billion people passed signals the way brain cells do, would the whole crowd wake up as one single mind?

name: The Inner Light
blurb: Picture a being that acts exactly like you yet feels nothing inside; if that's even thinkable, what lights you up?

name: The Hard Problem
blurb: We can trace every neuron firing and still not say why any of it feels like anything at all from within.

name: The Binding Problem
blurb: Color, shape, and sound are handled in scattered brain regions, so how do they snap into one seamless now?

name: Six Degrees
blurb: Pick any two people on Earth and a chain of roughly six friends-of-friends probably links them together.

name: The 80/20 Rule
blurb: Again and again about 80% of the results pour out of just 20% of the causes, a lopsided law of nearly everything.

name: Zipf's Law
blurb: In every language the most common word shows up twice as often as the second, thrice the third, like clockwork.

name: The Collatz Trip
blurb: Halve it if even, triple-plus-one if odd; start from any number and you always seem to spiral back down to one.

name: The Sunrise Problem
blurb: Having watched the sun rise every day of your life, what odds should you give it rises tomorrow? It's answerable.

name: The German Tank Problem
blurb: Spot a few serial numbers and you can cleverly estimate how many exist in all, guessing the whole from a glimpse.

name: The Party Theorem
blurb: In any room there's always at least one person such that, if they're smiling, then everyone is, provably so.

name: The Paradox of Horror
blurb: Why do we happily seek out stories that frighten us, paying to feel a fear we'd never want in real life?

name: The Mandelbrot Boundary
blurb: Zoom into the edge of one simple equation's picture and you fall through endless, never-repeating worlds.

name: Newton's Bucket
blurb: Spin a bucket of water alone in empty space and its surface still curves, so what is it even spinning against?
